parentQuestion: A patient undergoes surgery to remove a tumor that has been secreting excessive growth hormone directly into the bloodstream, causing gigantism. The tumor originated in which type of gland?

scaffolds.q: Growth hormone affects the entire body, not just one local area.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Growth hormone promotes growth of bones and tissues throughout the body - systemic effects.

scaffolds.q: Hormones travel through the BLOODSTREAM to reach their targets.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Hormones are chemical messengers that get released into blood and travel everywhere.

scaffolds.q: ENDOCRINE glands secrete hormones directly into the bloodstream.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! 'Endo' = inside. Endocrine glands are ductless - they dump hormones straight into blood.

scaffolds.q: The pituitary gland produces growth hormone and is an endocrine gland.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The pituitary is called the 'master gland' - it makes many hormones including growth hormone.

scaffolds.q: Gigantism results from too much growth hormone during childhood.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Excess GH before the growth plates close causes extreme height - gigantism.

scaffolds.q: A tumor in an endocrine gland can produce excess hormones continuously.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Tumors often retain the secretory function but lose the normal feedback control - overproduction.

scaffolds.q: EXOCRINE glands have ducts and deliver to surfaces - NOT into blood.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Sweat and salivary glands are exocrine. They don't cause systemic hormone effects like gigantism.

scaffolds.q: A tumor secreting hormones into blood must originate from an ENDOCRINE gland.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The fact that growth hormone is going directly into blood tells you it's endocrine tissue.

scaffolds.q: The answer is ENDOCRINE gland - secretes hormones directly into bloodstream.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not exocrine (has ducts), not holocrine/apocrine (secretion modes, not gland types). Endocrine = ductless hormone secretion.
